findings.description: SQL injection: user-controlled `email` is interpolated into the query via an f-string (the module otherwise uses parameterized queries), allowing authentication bypass or row forgery via UNION (CWE-89 / OWASP A03 Injection).

Inverted session-expiry check: `if session.expires_at > utcnow()` deletes/rejects LIVE sessions and the fall-through `return session.user_id` (line 109) accepts only EXPIRED ones, so expired tokens authenticate indefinitely and the TTL is never enforced (CWE-613 Insufficient Session Expiration).

Permanent account lockout / DoS: once `failed_attempts >= MAX_FAILED_ATTEMPTS` the account returns before any password check (line 72), `LOCKOUT_MINUTES` (line 17) is never used and there is no time-based reset, so an attacker who knows a victim's email can permanently lock the account with 5 bad guesses (CWE-645).

Username-enumeration timing side-channel: unknown emails return immediately (line 61) while known emails incur the 100k-iteration PBKDF2 hash (line 73), letting an attacker distinguish valid accounts by response time (CWE-203/CWE-208).

`find_user_by_email` runs `SELECT * FROM users` and linearly scans in Python, loading every row (and all password hashes/salts) into memory — an availability/DoS risk via resource exhaustion at scale, plus needless broad exposure of credential material (perf detail deferred to the performance critic).

Scanner flagged log injection here, but `user.id` is an integer from the DB (not raw user-supplied text), so CRLF/log-forging risk is minimal; reported for completeness per the exhaustive-reporting bar.
Scanner flagged log injection here; `session.user_id` is an integer, not attacker-controlled free text, so practical log-forging risk is low — reported for completeness.

`datetime.utcnow()` (also lines 105, 147) is deprecated in Python 3.12+ and returns a naive datetime; mixing naive/aware datetimes can later corrupt expiry comparisons — not a direct vuln but a latent correctness/security hazard for session timing.
